question: What was the problem and resolution for this bug Node.js issue: child_process 'spawn' event is emitted too soon?
ideal_answer:
Issue #37782 reports: child_process 'spawn' event is emitted too soon. Problem: Run the following simple repro example:

```console
$> node ./parent.mjs
```

<details>
<summary>parent.mjs</summary>

```js
import { fork } from 'child_process';


const subprocess = fork('./child.mjs');

subprocess.on('close', (...args) => { console.error('[PARENT] received', ...args) });
subprocess.on('disconnect', (...args) => { console.error('[PARENT] received', ...args) });
subprocess.on('error', (...args) => { console.error('[PARENT] received', ...args) });
subprocess.on('exit', (...args) => { console.error('[PARENT] received', ...args) });
subprocess.on('message', (...args) => { consol Observed behavior: Only parent's subprocess.on('message') is triggered:

```console
$> node ./parent.mjs
{ 'parent::subprocess.send': true }
[PARENT] received { hello: 'parent' }
```

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3012099/111513980-b0865500-8727-11eb-8d07-617170365ded.jpeg)

### Additional info

I installed node via nvm. I verified the version of node actually running is truly 15.11.0 by console Resolution evidence: Linked PR #3830: ci: fix e2e tests timing out. Resolution type: linked_pr. Maintainer context: JakobJingleheimer (MEMBER): Interestingly, if I wrap the subprocess.send() (still inside on('spawn')) in a setTimeout with _at least_ a 29 millisecond delay, it almost always works (~80–90% success rate).

The setTimeout trick outside on('spawn') works but needs a much larger number, like 3 seconds.

JakobJingleheimer (MEMBER): Yep, that's what I surmised.

The docs for [`Event: 'spawn'`](https://nodejs.org/api/child_process.html#child_process_event_spawn) say:
> the 'spawn' event is emitted once the child process has spawned successfully.

It would appear the doc is either incorrect or (more

label: bug
question: What was the problem and resolution for this bug Node.js issue: v18.18.2 -> v18.19.0, v20.5.1 -> v20.6.0 regression: pathToFileURL trims whitespace.?
ideal_answer:
Issue #51167 reports: v18.18.2 -> v18.19.0, v20.5.1 -> v20.6.0 regression: pathToFileURL trims whitespace.. Problem: ```js
import {pathToFileURL} from 'url';

const filename = `test-${String.fromCharCode(0)}`;
console.log(pathToFileURL(filename).toString()); // file:///[...]/test-
```

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

always

### What is the expected behavior? Why is that the expected behavior?

In Node v18.18.2 and v20.5.1:

```js
import {pathToFileURL} from 'url';

const filename = `test-${String.fromCharCode(0)}`;
console.log(pathToFileURL(filename).toString()); // file:///[...]/test-%00
```

### What do you see instead?

Where prior versions retained the trailing whitespac Expected behavior: In Node v18.18.2 and v20.5.1:

```js
import {pathToFileURL} from 'url';

const filename = `test-${String.fromCharCode(0)}`;
console.log(pathToFileURL(filename).toString()); // file:///[...]/test-%00
```

### What do you see instead?

Where prior versions retained the trailing whitespace and returned a pathname ending in `/test-%00`, starting in v18.19.0 and v20.6.0 the trailing whitespace is remov Observed behavior: Where prior versions retained the trailing whitespace and returned a pathname ending in `/test-%00`, starting in v18.19.0 and v20.6.0 the trailing whitespace is removed and results in a pathname ending in `/test-`.

### Additional information

This regression was introduced by optimizations in https://github.com/nodejs/node/pull/48709, specifically the [switch from](https://github.com/nodejs/node/ Resolution evidence: Linked PR #264: Use lts/* and lts/-n for node version; work around Node breaking change.. Resolution type: linked_pr. Maintainer context: anonrig (MEMBER): cc @nodejs/url

avivkeller (MEMBER): You can work on any issue you'd like. If you have a suggested improvement to any part of the code base, feel free to submit a PR.

dario-piotrowicz (MEMBER): It looks like this issue got resolved in v22.12, as you can see here:

![screenshot showing that the error can get reproduced in v22.11 but not v22.12](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ea782302-4a83-4714-bf3a-b3fb228b9782)

(and it still works as intended in v23) So I think that it is fine to close this issue

label: feature
question: What was the problem and resolution for this feature Node.js issue: Adding Websocket support to core?
ideal_answer:
Issue #19308 reports: Adding Websocket support to core. Problem: The original thread where adding this was discussed #1010 was closed with a decision by the iojs TC  to rather implement [lower level buffer methods](https://github.com/nodejs/node/pull/1202), but that was abandoned.

[There is an open EPS](https://github.com/nodejs/node-eps/pull/6) to add the feature, but we have since abandoned the process.

Some of the people who originally were -1 changed their opinions in #1010 more recently. In fact, we already ship a partial implementation of ws in the inspector.

I think it might be worth us revisiting adding WS to core.

/cc @eugeneo @rauchg Resolution evidence: Linked PR #6: ep: ws in core. Resolution type: linked_pr. Maintainer context: devsnek (MEMBER): i'm not wholly against this but i would like to factor in how much stuff we put into all our release binaries. if we can come up with more creative ways for shipping stuff like this i'm totally a +1 (#19307)

devsnek (MEMBER): @mscdex can you be explicit in your reasoning?

lpinca (MEMBER): As discussed in #1010 and as a maintainer of `ws` I'm +1 on adding WebSocket to core.

label: feature
question: What was the problem and resolution for this feature Node.js issue: `readFileSync` is 40% faster than `readFile` for large files?
ideal_answer:
Issue #41435 reports: `readFileSync` is 40% faster than `readFile` for large files. Problem: ### What is the problem this feature will solve?

make `readFile` as fast as `readFileSync`

### What is the feature you are proposing to solve the problem?

read the whole file in one operation to avoid excessive context switching

### What alternatives have you considered?

_No response_ Resolution evidence: Linked PR #41436: fs: `readFile` in one syscall to avoid context switching. Resolution type: linked_pr. Maintainer context: bnoordhuis (MEMBER): By some coincidence I was thinking about this earlier this week. The problem with `fs.readFile()` is that it round-trips through libuv's thread pool several times: open file, stat fd, read data, close fd.

For small files that adds considerable overhead. It's way more efficient to add a C++ `ReadFile()` method that does it all in one go through `uv_queue_work()`. Libuv's `uv_fs_*()` functions can be called synchronously and without an event loop.

(If and when libuv grows io_uring support, that might turn into a deoptimization again, but that's still in the future.)
notes: Draft auto-generated golden example. Manually review before final eval.

Issue #42183 reports: impl CookieStore?. Problem: ### What is the problem this feature will solve?

Cookie parsing and serializing is tuff. Most often you need a cookie plugin for whatever reason and dose are downloaded milion of times / week on npm

Adding a memory based [CookieStore](https://wicg.github.io/cookie-store) that can be constructed using `new CookieStore()` in some form would be beneficial. I'm just imagining http, https, http2 and [fetch](https://github.com/whatwg/fetch/issues/1384) to have support for passing in own custom CookieStore parameter somewhere for it to be used somehow Resolution evidence: Linked PR #1441: feat: implement `CookieStore`. Resolution type: linked_pr. Maintainer context: benjamingr (MEMBER): @jimmywarting the primary issue with something like this is that Node programs often serve multiple users (e.g. http servers) whereas web APIs are built for single-tenancy.

The web API doesn't currently allow `new CookieStore()` to set up an empty store and Node can't expose an equivalent of `document.cookie` (nor do any of the current APIs even support cookies for the abovementioned reason).

There definitely is the case for http _clients_ in Node finding this useful though given the web API doesn't expose `CookieStore` the way it does `EventTarget` not sure if that's ev

label: feature
question: What was the problem and resolution for this feature Node.js issue: Please add latest brotli 1.1.0 bindings with compression dictionary support?
ideal_answer:
Issue #52250 reports: Please add latest brotli 1.1.0 bindings with compression dictionary support. Problem: ### What is the problem this feature will solve?

Current node brotli compression does not support compression dictionary. 
The `dictionary` option only supported for deflate/inflate according to [documentation](https://nodejs.org/api/zlib.html#compressor-options):

> dictionary <Buffer> | <TypedArray> | <DataView> | <ArrayBuffer> (**deflate/inflate only**, empty dictionary by default)

This prevents using node.js directly to create compression dictionary diffs at build time which is necessary in order to adopt [Compression dictionary transport](https://github.com/WICG/compression-dictionary-t Resolution evidence: Linked PR #61763: zlib: add support for brotli compression dictionary. Resolution type: linked_pr. Maintainer context: benjamingr (MEMBER): @nodejs/zlib it looks like this just needs exposing in the API JS side

juanarbol (MEMBER): I can work on this :-)

MarshallOfSound (MEMBER): @juanarbol is this in flight somewhere, would love to poke at a dev branch or something 🤔 Poking at the [compression dictionary transport](https://github.com/WICG/compression-dictionary-transport) tech and built in bindings would make this a lot easier 😅 

Happy to help out with the change too 🥳
notes: Draft auto-generated golden example. Manually review before final eval.

ideal_answer:
Issue #53700 reports: Allow `Buffer.copy` to take an `ArrayBuffer`. Problem: ### What is the problem this feature will solve?

Avoid creating a temporary typed buffer:

```js
Buffer.from(arrayBuffer).copy(dst, 0)
```

```js
Buffer.copy(arrayBuffer, dst, 0)
```

### What alternatives have you considered?

_No response_ Resolution evidence: Linked PR #61168: Create `Buffer.copy` static method. Resolution type: linked_pr. Maintainer context: ronag (MEMBER): @nodejs/performance @joyeecheung

ronag (MEMBER): > @ronag will this Buffer.copy a new method or it is just buf.copy(target[, targetStart[, sourceStart[, sourceEnd]]]) function that will also start taking ArrayBuffer in the target parameter?

The idea is to be able to copy both to and from an ArrayBuffer so I would prefer a static method.

Qard (MEMBER): Might be a good idea to centralize all copying in that static method so the instance copy method delegates to the static method and it just handles copying of _all_ buffer varieties. 🤔

label: docs
question: What was the problem and resolution for this docs Node.js issue: http.IncomingMessage aborted Event isn't the same as http.IncomingMessage close Event?
ideal_answer:
Issue #43688 reports: http.IncomingMessage aborted Event isn't the same as http.IncomingMessage close Event. Problem: ### Affected URL(s)

https://nodejs.org/api/http.html#class-httpincomingmessage

### Description of the problem

The "`aborted`" event is deprecated and states "`Deprecated. Listen for 'close' event instead.`" which isn't related in any way.
The `close` event fires whenever the stream is `CLOSED` while the `aborted` event only fires if the request is `ABORTED`.

For exmaple a little HTTP-Server:
```js
// server.js
const http = require('http')

const server = http.createServer((request, response) => {
    request.on('error', (error) => {
        console.error('> THERE WAS AN ERROR', error)
     Resolution evidence: Linked PR #103: fix(web-incoming): close downstream stream when upstream SSE aborts. Resolution type: linked_pr. Maintainer context: benjamingr (MEMBER): cc @dr-js @ronag

benjamingr (MEMBER): I assumed that the idea was "don't rely on this code that does cleanup should'nt care if it was closed or aborted" _but_ I concede Robert's intent might have been to check `.readableEnded` or something similar

ronag (MEMBER): Yep, you should check in `'close'` whether or not the stream we prematurely closed.
notes: Draft auto-generated golden example. Manually review before final eval.

label: docs
question: What was the problem and resolution for this docs Node.js issue: Inaccurate sentence about the event loop tick and the process.nextTick?
ideal_answer:
Issue #51871 reports: Inaccurate sentence about the event loop tick and the process.nextTick. Problem: ### Affected URL(s)

https://nodejs.org/en/learn/asynchronous-work/understanding-processnexttick

### Description of the problem

"_**Every time the event loop takes a full trip, we call it a tick.**_" - this sentence is very inaccurate. The "**_event loop full trip_**" phrase looks like if we are talking about one event loop iteration from "timers" phase to "close callbacks" phase.

So the next sentence "_**When we pass a function to process.nextTick(), we instruct the engine to invoke this function at the end of the current operation, before the next event loop tick starts**_" looks like the Resolution evidence: Linked PR #7204: content: clarify `process.nextTick()` timing. Resolution type: linked_pr. Maintainer context: joyeecheung (MEMBER): A more accurate description may be "Every time the runtime calls back into JavaScript for an event, we call it a tick".

There are still some nuances, the next tick queue checks are all over the place and in some APIs multiple checks can be done for one OS event at different internal processing phases just because the native land needs to call back into JS. At the same time this is not done consistently whenever the native land wants to call back into JS because of the overhead. So the "event" here really means Node.js events which may or may not correspond to OS events..
notes: Draft auto-generated golden example. Manually review before final eval.

node -p process.pid --cpu-prof --cpu-prof-name 'CPU.${pid}.cpuprofile' && ls CPU*

outputs

always

Following the doc: https://nodejs.org/api/cli.html#--cpu-prof

> If --cpu-prof-name is not specified, the generated profile is named CPU.${yyyymmdd}.${hhmmss}.${pid}.${tid}.${seq}.cpuprofile.

I expect to be able to use those placeholder when I specify a name, especially when I use the `cluster` module to avoid h Expected behavior: Following the doc: https://nodejs.org/api/cli.html#--cpu-prof

> If --cpu-prof-name is not specified, the generated profile is named CPU.${yyyymmdd}.${hhmmss}.${pid}.${tid}.${seq}.cpuprofile.

I expect to be able to use those placeholder when I specify a name, especially when I use the `cluster` module to avoid having all the profiling overriding each others.

I expected the filename to be `CPU.43 Observed behavior: ```sh
'CPU.${pid}.cpuprofile'
```

### Additional information

_No response_ Resolution evidence: Linked PR #57433: doc: clarify --cpu-prof-name allowed values. Resolution type: linked_pr. Maintainer context: joyeecheung (MEMBER): It looks like either a misunderstanding of what the doc says, or that the doc not being clear enough that the argument is not a pattern that will be filled by Node.js. The doc specifically says:

> If --cpu-prof-name is not specified, ...

which is not to be interpreted as "if it IS specified, Node.js will fill in the blank using a user-provided pattern". Rather, it means "if it IS specified, the name provided will be used as-is".

label: docs
question: What was the problem and resolution for this docs Node.js issue: Docs Incorrectly State setImmediate() Runs Before Timers?
ideal_answer:
Issue #57993 reports: Docs Incorrectly State setImmediate() Runs Before Timers. Problem: ### Affected URL(s)

https://nodejs.org/en/learn/asynchronous-work/discover-promises-in-nodejs#advanced-promise-methods

### Description of the problem

[When to Use Each](https://nodejs.org/en/learn/asynchronous-work/discover-promises-in-nodejs#when-to-use-each)
Use queueMicrotask() for tasks that need to run immediately after the current script and before any I/O or timer callbacks, typically for Promise resolutions.
Use process.nextTick() for tasks that should execute before any I/O events, often useful for deferring operations or handling errors synchronously.
Use setImmediate() for tasks  Resolution evidence: Linked PR #7677: fix(learn): improve `setImmediate` in Promises article. Resolution type: linked_pr. Maintainer context: avivkeller (MEMBER): Thank's for pointing that out! If you'd like to see a change in the learn articles, feel free to submit a PR to nodejs/nodejs.org.

label: question
question: What was the problem and resolution for this question Node.js issue: Missing stack traces from async functions after the first await?
ideal_answer:
Issue #11865 reports: Missing stack traces from async functions after the first await. Problem: Version: v7.7.3
Platform: Windows 7x64

The purpose of this issue is really a more broad request for better stack traces with async/await but I figured I would start with a very specific case. If we need to broaden the description, I'm good with that. I've seen a number of lengthy discussion on this subject in various places but I don't see any actual issues for it so I thought I'd start one and hopefully it's not just a duplicate of something I missed.

I'm filing the specific issue here because it seems the async/await functionality just added provides less useful error handling than we coul Resolution evidence: Linked PR #72: TypeScript then babel. Resolution type: linked_pr. Maintainer context: richardlau (MEMBER): Cc @nodejs/diagnostics

hashseed (MEMBER): That's because Error.stack only reflects the actual stack. The inspector provides async stack traces, but Error.stack will never include the async parts due to the overhead of collecting it.

hashseed (MEMBER): You could run node with --inspect and enable async stack trace in DevTools.

ideal_answer:

* **Version Resolution evidence: Linked PR #16402: https: add extra options to Agent#getName(). Resolution type: linked_pr. Maintainer context: bnoordhuis (MEMBER): #15206 is indeed the PR that is responsible for this change.  I assume you don't pass an explicit `ecdhCurve` option to `https.request()`?

It sounds like #15206 slipped in an accidental bug fix because `ecdhCurve` is supposed to default to `tls.DEFAULT_ECDH_CURVE`, which is `prime256v1` at the time of writing.  `secp384r1` shouldn't have worked without a manual override.  Regrettably, we don't seem to have test coverage for that.

@nodejs/security What do we want to do?

question: What was the problem and resolution for this question Node.js issue: Feature request: url.join(baseUrl, ...others)?
ideal_answer:
Issue #18288 reports: Feature request: url.join(baseUrl, ...others). Problem: <!--
Thank you for reporting an issue.

This issue tracker is for bugs and issues found within Node.js core.
If you require more general support please file an issue on our help
repo. https://github.com/nodejs/help


Please fill in as much of the template below as you're able.

Version: output of `node -v`
Platform: output of `uname -a` (UNIX), or version and 32 or 64-bit (Windows)
Subsystem: if known, please specify affected core module name

If possible, please provide code that demonstrates the problem, keeping it as
simple and free of external dependencies as you are able.
-->

* **Version Resolution evidence: Linked PR #6: Allow canonical_base to contain a path. Resolution type: linked_pr. Maintainer context: TimothyGu (MEMBER): How does this differ from `[baseUrl, ...others].join('/')`?

In any case, we are trying to move away from legacy URL APIs like `url.resolve()` in favor of the [WHATWG standard-compliant API](https://nodejs.org/api/url.html#url_the_whatwg_url_api). We probably will not consider this addition.

TimothyGu (MEMBER): `http://nodejs.org//foo/bar//baz/` is a valid URL that may have a different meaning from `http://nodejs.org/foo/bar/baz/`. It would be semantically incorrect to disallow this distinction.

label: question
question: What was the problem and resolution for this question Node.js issue: Can we supress the new experimental warning from the new default require(esm) setting??
ideal_answer:
Issue #55417 reports: Can we supress the new experimental warning from the new default require(esm) setting?. Problem: ### What is the problem this feature will solve?

I use NVM for Windows.

1. I run `nvm install 23.0.0`
2. `v23.0.0` gets installed.
3. I then run `nvm use 23.0.0`
4. I then run `npm install npm@latest -g`

The latest npm gets installed, but I also get the following output pollution:

```
(node:32728) ExperimentalWarning: Support for loading ES Module in require() is an experimental feature and might change at any time
(Use `node --trace-warnings ...` to show where the warning was created)
```

5. If I then run `npm install svgo -g`, I get the same `ExperimentalWarning` pollution.

Is there an Resolution evidence: Linked PR #56194: module: only emit require(esm) warning under --trace-require-module. Resolution type: linked_pr. Maintainer context: targos (MEMBER): You can use [`node --no-deprecation`](https://nodejs.org/docs/latest/api/cli.html#--no-deprecation) or `export NODE_OPTIONS="--no-deprecation"`.

targos (MEMBER): Or disable `require('esm')` with `--no-experimental-require-module`

targos (MEMBER): I don't know much about Windows, sorry.
notes: Draft auto-generated golden example. Manually review before final eval.

Maintainer context: bnoordhuis (MEMBER): Start node with `--perf_basic_prof` and attach perf with `perf top -g -p <pid>` once node is running.  The gettimeofday system calls are almost certainly `new Date()` or `Date.now()` calls in JS code.
